summary: MARTA's new CQ400 trains officially launch for passengers today, marking the most significant transit upgrade in decades. Georgia Power customers will see bills drop about $4 a month starting in June. The city budget fight heats up as residents demand parks funding over police increases. Stord hits $3 billion valuation with a $250 million raise. Little Tart expands to Candler Park with a new Parisian sister concept. And Georgia Tech baseball hosts an NCAA regional after coach James Ramsey earns a five-year extension.

topStory.headline: MARTA's New CQ400 Trains Launch for Passengers Today
topStory.summary: After years of development and testing, MARTA's cutting-edge CQ400 railcars officially enter revenue service, bringing what the agency calls 'the most technologically advanced trains in the U.S.' to Atlanta's rail network just weeks before the World Cup.
topStory.body: The wait is over for Atlanta transit riders. MARTA is officially launching its new CQ400 railcars for passenger service today, following a ceremonial first ride at Avondale Station. The Switzerland-built Stadler trains have been undergoing testing in Atlanta since early 2025, with several earlier launch timelines coming and going. The ceremonial first ride replicated the route of MARTA's famous inaugural journey in 1979, running between Avondale and Georgia State stations. The timing is fortuitous, with the FIFA World Cup arriving in Atlanta on June 11. The new trains feature modern amenities, improved accessibility, and enhanced safety systems that MARTA says represent the most significant upgrade to its rail fleet in decades. The launch comes amid scrutiny from an AJC investigation this week indicating that critical safety tests had not been completed by Stadler with weeks to go before the World Cup. MARTA's interim general manager and CEO Jonathan Hunt defended the readiness of the fleet. Multiple trains are expected to enter revenue service throughout the day across the Downtown and Midtown corridors.
topStory.imageAlt: New MARTA CQ400 train at station platform

stories.headline: Little Tart and New Parisian Concept Midi Coming to Candler Park
stories.summary: The beloved bakeshop is expanding to its fourth location, joined by a new sandwich shop inspired by Parisian cafes, in the space formerly occupied by Kelly's Closet bridal shop.
stories.body: Little Tart Bakeshop is bringing its pastries to Candler Park this fall, with founder Sarah O'Brien announcing a fourth location at 1643 McLendon Ave. NE. But that's only half the story. The team is also taking over the neighboring unit for a sister concept called Midi, a sandwich shop inspired by O'Brien's love of Parisian cafes. "I see these new spaces as a culmination of it all, a true distillation of what we want to eat, and thus what we want to make for you," O'Brien wrote on Instagram. "America is my country, but I have two hometowns. I love them both with my whole heart, and can't wait to bring a little bit more of my affection for Paris to Atlanta." Midi will offer sandwiches made with homemade bread and local ingredients, along with salads, small bites, and a wine and cocktail program. The concept will also incorporate elements of O'Brien's Big Softie ice cream brand. Both spots are expected to open this fall. Little Tart currently operates locations in Grant Park, Krog Street Market, and Westside Provisions District.

stories.headline: BeltLine Affordable Housing Redevelopment Plans Draw Neighbor Pushback
stories.summary: Boston-based Wingate Companies plans to purchase and redevelop Trestletree Village apartments near the BeltLine's newest section, with some neighbors concerned about density changes.
stories.body: A longtime affordable housing community adjacent to the Atlanta BeltLine's newest section could see major changes as Boston-based Wingate Companies moves to purchase the Trestletree Village Apartments. The two Trestletree communities, split between Grant Park and Ormewood Park, count a total of 188 units. Wingate, known locally as the developer behind City Lights in Old Fourth Ward, plans to close on both parcels this summer. According to neighborhood sources, Wingate intends to relocate all affordable Section 8 tenants to the southern Trestletree location, then raze the northern site for redevelopment. The proposal calls for three-story apartment buildings ringing the site along with a new parking deck. Some neighbors have expressed concern that denser housing could change the area's character. The northern site sits at 794 Ormewood Ave., bordered by an elevated BeltLine section, Ormewood Avenue, Eloise Street, and Mercer Street. Wingate has not yet submitted formal development plans to the city.
stories.imageAlt: Trestletree Village apartments near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Atlantans Pack Budget Hearing to Demand Parks Funding Over Police Increases
stories.summary: Nearly 30 residents braved a torrential downpour to speak against cuts to parks while police spending grows, but only four of 15 council members showed up to listen.
stories.body: Almost 30 Atlantans showed up to City Hall during a torrential downpour to voice concerns about the proposed city budget, but found mostly empty chairs waiting for them. Of 15 council members, only Jason Winston, Kelsea Bond, Matt Westmoreland, and Council President Marci Collier Overstreet attended the May 20 community budget session, the public's only dedicated hearing on the $995 million general fund budget for FY 2027. "Thank you to the four of you who showed up today. I know that most of us didn't come here to speak to a bunch of empty chairs," said resident Steven Imle, before criticizing a $16.5 million increase in police spending. The Atlanta Police Department is set to receive $324.4 million, making up nearly one-third of the general fund. Meanwhile, residents voiced concerns about a $2.7 million cut to the Parks and Recreation Department budget, which comprises just 5.5% of the general fund at $54.7 million. "A budget cut to parks is a budget cut to full community transformation," said Coreen Dent, president of a neighborhood organization in Downtown.

stories.headline: Georgia Power Bills to Drop About $4 a Month Starting in June
stories.summary: The Public Service Commission approved a rate decrease that will save customers an average of $50 a year, even as the utility works to recover $912 million in storm costs.
stories.body:
Georgia Power customers will catch a break on their electric bills starting next month.

The Georgia Public Service Commission unanimously approved a plan to lower overall rates, saving customers an average of $4.04 a month or about $50 a year. The rate decrease goes into effect in June, just as summer heat typically drives up energy use.

"Today's vote by the Georgia PSC will bring lower rates and real savings for Georgia families and businesses as the heat of summer begins and energy use increases," said Georgia Power CFO Tyler Cook.

The rate adjustment was part of two filings the utility submitted earlier this year to recover fuel costs for operating power plants and costs from storm restoration. Georgia Power's storm reserve is currently under-recovered by $912 million, with nearly $800 million stemming from Hurricane Helene damage in 2024, which the company describes as the most destructive storm in its history.

The utility plans to recover those storm costs over the next four years while still delivering the immediate rate relief.

stories.headline: Atlanta's Stord Hits $3 Billion Valuation with $250 Million Raise
stories.summary: The Georgia Tech-born logistics company closes its Series F round backed by Strike Capital, Kleiner Perkins, and Founders Fund, cementing its position as a pillar of Atlanta's tech scene.
stories.body: A decade after Hypepotamus first covered a scrappy Georgia Tech startup pitching storage solutions, Stord has become a titan of Atlanta's tech ecosystem. The logistics and supply chain company announced a $250 million Series F funding round at a $3 billion valuation, coming just a year after raising $200 million. The round was backed by existing investors including Strike Capital, Kleiner Perkins, Founders Fund, Franklin Templeton, Baillie Gifford, G Squared, Bond, and Lux. "Commerce infrastructure gets built once," said John Lagomarsino of Strike Capital. "From our earliest conversations with Sean Henry, it was clear Stord was assembling something rare: software, physical infrastructure, and AI combined in a way that turns fulfillment into a competitive advantage rather than a cost center." Stord now operates more than 100 facilities worldwide. The company is also rolling out a new Physical Intelligence Lab to advance its AI-powered fulfillment capabilities. The raise positions Stord as one of the most valuable private companies to emerge from Atlanta's startup scene.

stories.headline: Emory Names COO Christopher Augostini as Next President
stories.summary: The university's executive vice president and chief operating officer was unanimously elected by the Board of Trustees, ending a national search that included over 40 listening sessions.
stories.body: Emory University has promoted from within for its next leader, naming Christopher L. Augostini as its 22nd president effective September 1, 2026. Augostini, currently Emory's executive vice president and chief operating officer, was unanimously elected by the Board of Trustees following a national search that included more than 40 listening sessions and extensive feedback from students, faculty, staff, and alumni. "I'm extraordinarily honored and thrilled to be named Emory's next president," Augostini said. "As I look across the higher education landscape, I believe Emory is uniquely positioned to lead." Board Chair Robert C. Goddard III praised Augostini's "exceptional leadership skills, distinguished record of achievement, and deep understanding of Emory's culture and community." The appointment caps a career in higher education administration for Augostini, who has overseen Emory's operations during a period of significant growth and change. The university, located in Druid Hills, is one of the nation's leading research institutions.
stories.imageAlt: Christopher L. Augostini, Emory University's new president

stories.headline: Decatur School Board Launches Investigation Amid Podcast Controversy
stories.summary: The City Schools of Decatur announced an independent investigation following controversy over podcasts featuring the superintendent and her husband.
stories.body: The City Schools of Decatur Board of Education has announced an independent investigation following a growing controversy over podcasts featuring Superintendent and her husband. The announcement comes as pressure mounts from parents and community members who were already at odds with the board over a proposed Early Childhood Learning Center and district finances. Parents have demanded accountability over remarks made in the podcasts, with some calling for transparency about the superintendent's conduct. The controversy has intensified existing tensions within the district. The board did not specify the scope of the investigation or provide a timeline for its completion. Community members have packed recent board meetings to voice concerns. The podcast controversy adds to challenges facing the suburban school district, which serves approximately 5,800 students across 10 schools.
stories.imageAlt: City Schools of Decatur administration building

stories.headline: US Men's National Team Training in Georgia Ahead of World Cup
stories.summary: The most diverse USMNT roster in history is preparing at the Arthur M. Blank training center before friendlies against Senegal and Germany, with eight Black American-born players on the 26-man squad.
stories.body: The United States Men's National Team is in Georgia this week, preparing for the biggest tournament in American soccer history at the Arthur M. Blank U.S. Soccer National Training Center in Fayetteville. Head coach Mauricio Pochettino and the 26-man roster are training before friendlies against Senegal in Charlotte on Sunday and Germany in Chicago on June 6, their final tune-ups before the World Cup kicks off June 11. This year's roster includes more Black American-born players than any before. Midfielders Tyler Adams and Weston McKennie join forwards Haji Wright and Tim Weah, along with defenders Alex Freeman, Mark McKenzie, Chris Richards, and former Atlanta United star Miles Robinson. "The red, white, and blue draped over the backs of the American players is an honest representation of this country," noted The Atlanta Voice. Atlanta's Mercedes-Benz Stadium (officially "Atlanta Stadium" for tournament branding) will host eight World Cup matches starting June 15. The team's presence at the Blank training facility underscores Atlanta's central role in hosting the tournament.
stories.imageAlt: USMNT training at Arthur M. Blank facility

stories.headline: Georgia Tech Baseball Signs Coach James Ramsey to Five-Year Extension
stories.summary: After leading the Yellow Jackets to a historic 48-9 regular season and ACC Tournament title, the former Atlanta-area product locks in through 2031.
stories.body: Georgia Tech head baseball coach James Ramsey has earned a contract extension after a historic first season, signing a new five-year deal that keeps him with the program through 2031. Ramsey, who grew up in metro Atlanta, led the Yellow Jackets to a 48-9 regular season record, the highest winning percentage (84.2%) in program history. The team captured the ACC regular season title and the program's first ACC Tournament championship since 2014. "We are trying to be the type of program that makes Tech fans proud on and off the field, winning at a high level, together, with toughness and discipline, for many more years to come," Ramsey said. Athletic Director Ryan Alpert praised the hire: "I feel that we have the best coach in all of college baseball in James Ramsey." No. 2 national seed Georgia Tech hosts the Atlanta regional this weekend at Russ Chandler Stadium in Midtown, facing Illinois-Chicago in Friday's opener. Game times remain unchanged despite weather concerns affecting other regionals around the country.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia Tech baseball coach James Ramsey

stories.headline: Inside LaRayia's Bodega: The Brooklyn Transplant Bringing Plant-Based Magic to Ponce City Market
stories.summary: LaRayia Gaston describes Atlanta as 'a city full of fairies' and has built one of the city's most beloved hidden gems over five years at the Central Food Hall.
stories.body: When LaRayia Gaston booked a flight to Atlanta during the height of COVID-19, she had never visited the city before. Now, five years later, her plant-based grab-and-go eatery at Ponce City Market has become one of Old Fourth Ward's most quietly beloved hidden gems. "I just thought it was magical," Gaston told The Atlanta Voice of her first impressions. "It was literally a city full of fairies." The Brooklyn-born entrepreneur's path to Atlanta was anything but linear. She launched a clothing brand in New York, built a showroom in Los Angeles, and worked in fashion while witnessing the devastation of Skid Row. "When you start to see those things at a level of devastation, it kind of makes you question your art and what you do, and how much of it has impact," she said. That questioning led to LaRayia's Bodega, tucked into a corner of Ponce City Market's Central Food Hall. The spot serves plant-based meals with soul, building a loyal following among locals who've discovered its charms. "Something called me to this corner," Gaston said of finding her space at PCM.

stories.headline: Georgia Drought Eases Slightly But Nearly 80% Still in Extreme or Exceptional Conditions
stories.summary: Recent rains have helped, with drought coverage dropping from 80% to 56% in the most severe categories, but officials warn the state needs much more precipitation heading into summer.
stories.body:
Georgia remains gripped by drought as the state heads into summer, though recent rains have provided some relief.

The latest U.S. Drought Monitor shows 100% of Georgia is still experiencing some level of drought, with 56% of the state in extreme or exceptional drought conditions. That's down from 80% in the most severe categories last week.

Since Friday, more than three inches of rainfall has been recorded in Atlanta, more than what the city saw from March 15 through May 21 combined. But officials say much more rain is needed to erase the deficit.

"Drought conditions continue to exist across the state. A Level 1 Drought Response is the appropriate action at this time," said Katie Bloomfield, director of external affairs for the Georgia Environmental Protection Division.

The Level 1 declaration, in effect since April 27, requires public water utilities to inform customers about conservation and report monthly water withdrawals. The EPD continues to monitor whether higher drought response levels will be needed.

Flash flood threats return this weekend as waves of rain move through, potentially bringing 1-2 inches of additional rainfall.
